result:
I would end the cycle of surprise—whenever mother nature or human ambition invent something that could upset the balance, every person would immediately know three things:  
1) what it is,  
2) what it can do, and  
3) what it might cost.

Not telepathy or forced obedience—just perfect, instant, inclusive transparency. A fragile coral reef “sees” when a new toxin is flushed into it and transmits the fact to fishers, regulators, and poets. A biotech lab “hears” the moment its strands of DNA start shaping a pathogen, and the discovery flows automatically to neighboring kitchens as well as to the World Health Organization. The black box closes; the open box blinks awake.

Good intuitions normally travel too slowly to weigh against panic, profit, or propaganda. With surprise removed, the same restless human creativity that now amplifies inequality and disaster would immediately face the total screaming audience of possible victims and possible beneficiaries. Some reckless choices will still be tried—people have always been stubborn—but they will no longer win simply because no one knew the game had started.

In that one changed rule, the rest—climate, injustice, war, even loneliness—starts to look soluble, because the solutions were already obvious to scattered minds who simply didn’t have the moment of shared attention.

prompt: If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
